title: kiro-prompt
description:
## Problem
The real kiro-cli integration test (TestSessionToKiro_RealKiro) skips because:
1. kiro-cli's prompt doesn't match the parser's promptRegex
2. Current regex: `(?m)^(>|$|kiro>|claude>)\s*$`
3. MCP servers take ~10-15 seconds to initialize

## Investigation Findings
- kiro-cli outputs streaming data during startup
- The actual prompt pattern needs to be captured from real kiro-cli output
- Q_TERM environment variable is set correctly
- Startup timeout already increased to 60s

## Tasks
1. Run kiro-cli manually and capture the exact prompt pattern after initialization
2. Update promptRegex in internal/kiro/output_parser.go to match
3. Consider if we need different parsing strategy for kiro-cli vs other CLIs
4. Ensure TestSessionToKiro_RealKiro passes when KIRO_INTEGRATION_TEST=1

## Files
- internal/kiro/output_parser.go (promptRegex)
- internal/kiro/process.go (startup handling)
- internal/integration/session_kiro_test.go (TestSessionToKiro_RealKiro)

title: Create ObservableProcess wrapper with PTY output multiplexing

title: Fix flaky concurrent test in observer_registry_test.go
close_reason: Fixed flaky concurrent test by (1) making a copy of the observers map while holding the lock in Broadcast method to avoid iterating over a map that could be modified concurrently, (2) adding sync.Once to Observer.Close to prevent double-close panics, (3) not closing SendChan in Close method to avoid race conditions with concurrent sends - only Done channel is closed to signal observer shutdown, and (4) adding Observer.Send method that safely checks Done channel before sending

title: Epic: Phase 3 - Session Management
description:
Implement session lifecycle management with SQLite persistence.

## Scope
- Session entity and SessionID type
- SQLite store for session persistence
- SessionManager for lifecycle operations
- Automatic cleanup of idle sessions

## Key Design
**Session Model**: Thread timestamp = Session ID
- New message (no thread): Creates new session
- Thread reply: Continues existing session
- Each session has its own Kiro directory

## Key Interfaces
```go
type SessionID string // Thread timestamp

type Session struct {
    ID             SessionID
    ChannelID      string
    ThreadTS       string
    UserID         string
    KiroSessionDir string
    CreatedAt      time.Time
    LastActivityAt time.Time
    Status         SessionStatus
}

type SessionStore interface {
    Get(ctx, id SessionID) (*Session, error)
    Save(ctx, session *Session) error
    Delete(ctx, id SessionID) error
    List(ctx) ([]*Session, error)
    ListByUser(ctx, userID string) ([]*Session, error)
}

type SessionManager interface {
    GetOrCreate(ctx, channelID, threadTS, userID string) (*Session, isNew bool, err error)
    Get(ctx, id SessionID) (*Session, error)
    UpdateActivity(ctx, id SessionID) error
    Close(ctx, id SessionID) error
    Cleanup(ctx) error
}
```

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 1 Foundation complete

## Parallelization
- Session entity and store can be parallel
- Manager depends on store
close_reason: Phase 3 complete: Session entity, SQLite store, SessionManager implemented.

title: Implement SQLite session store
description:
Implement SessionStore using SQLite for persistence.

## Design
```go
// internal/session/sqlite_store.go

type SQLiteStore struct {
    db     *sql.DB
    logger *zap.Logger
}

func NewSQLiteStore(dbPath string, logger *zap.Logger) (*SQLiteStore, error)

// Schema
/*
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sessions (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    channel_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    thread_ts TEXT NOT NULL,
    user_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    kiro_session_dir TEXT NOT NULL,
    created_at DATETIME NOT NULL,
    last_activity_at DATETIME NOT NULL,
    status INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
);

CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_user_id ON sessions(user_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_status ON sessions(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_last_activity ON sessions(last_activity_at);
*/

func (s *SQLiteStore) Get(ctx context.Context, id SessionID) (*Session, error)
func (s *SQLiteStore) Save(ctx context.Context, session *Session) error
func (s *SQLiteStore) Delete(ctx context.Context, id SessionID) error
func (s *SQLiteStore) List(ctx context.Context) ([]*Session, error)
func (s *SQLiteStore) ListByUser(ctx context.Context, userID string) ([]*Session, error)
func (s *SQLiteStore) ListIdle(ctx context.Context, idleSince time.Time) ([]*Session, error)
func (s *SQLiteStore) Close() error
```

## Features
1. Auto-create database and tables on init
2. Use prepared statements for performance
3. Handle concurrent access (SQLite supports this)
4. Proper error wrapping

## Files
- internal/session/sqlite_store.go
- internal/session/sqlite_store_test.go

## Test Strategy
- Use in-memory SQLite (:memory:) for unit tests
- Test all CRUD operations
- Test concurrent access
- Test error cases

## Acceptance Criteria
- Store creates database if not exists
- All CRUD operations work
- Indexes improve query performance
- Unit tests with in-memory SQLite pass
close_reason: SQLite session store implemented with all CRUD operations. 16 tests pass including concurrent access tests.

title: Epic: Phase 4 - Kiro Bridge
description:
Implement PTY-based interface to Kiro CLI.

## Scope
- PTY process management for Kiro CLI
- Output parser (ANSI removal, completion detection)
- Streaming response callback
- Process lifecycle management

## Key Challenge
Kiro CLI is interactive terminal app. We use PTY (pseudo-terminal) to:
1. Spawn kiro-cli as if in real terminal
2. Send messages via stdin
3. Capture streaming output from stdout
4. Parse output, detect when response is complete

## Key Interfaces
```go
type ResponseHandler func(chunk string, isComplete bool) error

type Bridge interface {
    Start(ctx context.Context) error
    SendMessage(ctx context.Context, message string, handler ResponseHandler) error
    IsRunning() bool
    Close() error
}
```

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 1 Foundation complete
- Uses github.com/creack/pty library

## Challenges & Mitigations
1. **Output parsing**: ANSI removal + prompt detection + timeout fallback
2. **Process lifecycle**: Process groups, graceful shutdown
3. **Streaming**: Read loop with non-blocking approach

## Parallelization
- Process management and output parser can be parallel
- Tests depend on both
close_reason: Phase 4 complete: Output parser, Kiro PTY process, Bridge interface implemented.

Wire all components together and implement end-to-end tests.

## Scope

- Integration tests (component interactions)

## Key Deliverables
1. **main.go**: Complete application wiring
2. **Retry logic**: Auto-retry once on Kiro failure
3. **Integration tests**: Test component boundaries
4. **E2E tests**: Real Slack + Kiro testing
5. **README**: Setup and usage documentation

## Dependencies
- Requires all previous phases complete
- This is the final assembly phase

## Test Strategy
- Integration tests: Mock external deps, test component wiring
- E2E tests: Real Slack workspace (test channel)
- Skip E2E in CI unless explicitly enabled

## Parallelization
- main.go and retry logic sequential
- Integration tests and E2E tests can be parallel after main.go
- Documentation can be parallel
close_reason: Phase 6 complete: main.go wired, retry bridge implemented, graceful shutdown working. All tests pass.

title: Setup structured logging with zap
description:
Implement structured logging using uber-go/zap.

## Design
```go
// internal/logging/logger.go

func NewLogger(cfg *config.LoggingConfig) (*zap.Logger, error)

// Helper to add common fields
func WithSessionID(logger *zap.Logger, sessionID string) *zap.Logger
func WithUserID(logger *zap.Logger, userID string) *zap.Logger
func WithChannelID(logger *zap.Logger, channelID string) *zap.Logger
```

## Features
1. Configurable log level (debug/info/warn/error)
2. Configurable format (json for prod, console for dev)
3. Context fields for request tracing
4. Performance-optimized (zap's zero-allocation design)

## Files
- internal/logging/logger.go
- internal/logging/logger_test.go

## Log Format (JSON)
```json
{
  "level": "info",
  "ts": "2024-01-15T10:30:00.000Z",
  "caller": "slack/handler.go:42",
  "msg": "Processing message",
  "session_id": "1234567890.123456",
  "user_id": "U456",
  "channel_id": "C123"
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Logger respects config level/format
- JSON output in prod mode
- Console output in dev mode
- Helper functions add context correctly
close_reason: Structured logging with zap implemented. All 6 tests pass. Supports JSON/console formats.

```go
type SlackClient interface {
    PostMessage(ctx, channelID, text string, opts ...Option) (ts string, err error)

}
```

- message (im): Direct message to bot

- Handler setup depends on client wrapper

title: Epic: Phase 5 - Streaming Updates
description:
Implement progressive Slack message updates.

## Scope
- Output buffer with debouncing
- Stream updater for Slack messages
- Thinking indicators during processing

## Key Design
As Kiro responds, progressively update the Slack message:
1. Post initial 'Thinking...' placeholder
2. Update message as chunks arrive (debounced)
3. Finalize with complete response

## Key Interfaces
```go
type StreamUpdater interface {
    Start(ctx, channelID, threadTS string) (messageTS string, err error)
    Update(ctx, content string) error
    Complete(ctx, finalContent string) error
    Error(ctx, err error) error
}
```

## Debouncing Strategy
- Buffer output chunks
- Update Slack at most every 500ms (configurable)
- Prevents hitting Slack rate limits
- Provides smoother UX

## Dependencies
- Requires Phase 1 Foundation complete
- Uses Slack client from Phase 2

## Parallelization
- Buffer and streamer can be parallel
- Tests depend on both
close_reason: Phase 5 complete: OutputBuffer with debouncing and Streamer for progressive Slack updates. 21 tests pass.

title: Create Makefile with build targets
description:
Create Makefile for common development tasks.

## Targets
```makefile
.PHONY: build test lint run clean deps mocks

# Build the server binary
build:
	go build -o bin/server ./cmd/server

# Run all tests with coverage
test:
	go test -v -race -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
	go tool cover -html=coverage.out -o coverage.html

# Run linter
lint:
	golangci-lint run ./...

# Run the server
run:
	go run ./cmd/server

# Clean build artifacts
clean:
	rm -rf bin/ coverage.out coverage.html

# Download dependencies
deps:
	go mod download
	go mod tidy

# Generate mocks
mocks:
	go generate ./...

# Integration tests (requires kiro-cli)
test-integration:
	go test -v -tags=integration ./...

# E2E tests (requires Slack test workspace)
test-e2e:
	E2E_TEST=true go test -v -tags=e2e ./internal/e2e/...
```

## Files
- Makefile

## Acceptance Criteria
- make build creates binary
- make test runs unit tests
- make lint runs linter (install golangci-lint if needed)
- make run starts the server
close_reason: Makefile created with build/test/lint/run targets. make build verified working.

title: Implement configuration management with viper
description:
Create configuration loading system using viper.

## Design
Config struct hierarchy:
```go
type Config struct {
    Slack      SlackConfig      
    Kiro       KiroConfig       
    Session    SessionConfig    
    Streaming  StreamingConfig  
    Logging    LoggingConfig    
}

type SlackConfig struct {
    BotToken  string        // xoxb-... (required)
    AppToken  string        // xapp-... (required)
    DebugMode bool          // default: false
}

type KiroConfig struct {
    BinaryPath      string        // default: kiro-cli
    SessionBasePath string        // default: /tmp/kiro-sessions
    StartupTimeout  time.Duration // default: 30s
    ResponseTimeout time.Duration // default: 120s
    MaxRetries      int           // default: 1
}

type SessionConfig struct {
    IdleTimeout      time.Duration // default: 30m
    MaxSessionsTotal int           // default: 100
    MaxSessionsUser  int           // default: 5
    DatabasePath     string        // default: /tmp/kiro-agent/sessions.db
}

type StreamingConfig struct {
    UpdateInterval time.Duration // default: 500ms
}

type LoggingConfig struct {
    Level  string // debug, info, warn, error
    Format string // json, console
}
```

## Features
1. Load from YAML file (configs/config.yaml)
2. Environment variable overrides (KIRO_AGENT_* prefix)
3. Sensible defaults for all optional fields
4. Validation on load (required fields, format checks)

## Files
- internal/config/config.go
- internal/config/config_test.go
- configs/config.example.yaml

## Acceptance Criteria
- Config loads from YAML
- Env vars override YAML values
- Validation fails fast on missing required fields
- Unit tests pass
close_reason: Config management implemented with viper. All 6 tests pass. Supports YAML + env var overrides.

title: Write documentation and README
description:

## Deliverables

### README.md
```markdown
# Amelia Slack Agent

Slack bot that bridges messages to Kiro CLI agent.

## Features
- DM and @mention support
- Thread-based conversation sessions
- Streaming responses
- SQLite session persistence
- Auto-retry on failures

## Prerequisites
- Go 1.22+
- kiro-cli installed
- Slack app with Socket Mode enabled

## Quick Start
1. Clone repository
2. Copy configs/config.example.yaml to configs/config.yaml
3. Set SLACK_BOT_TOKEN and SLACK_APP_TOKEN
4. Run: make run

## Configuration
See configs/config.example.yaml for all options.

## Development
- make build - Build binary
- make test - Run unit tests
- make lint - Run linter
- make test-integration - Run integration tests

## Architecture
[Link to PLAN.md or architecture diagram]
```

### configs/config.example.yaml
- Full example with comments
- All configurable options documented

### Architecture Documentation
- Component diagram
- Message flow diagram
- Session lifecycle diagram

- README.md
- configs/config.example.yaml
- docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (optional)

## Acceptance Criteria
- README covers setup and usage
- Example config is complete
- All configuration options documented
- Quick start guide works

Created docs/ARCHITECTURE.md with detailed technical architecture including system design, component descriptions, message flow diagrams, session lifecycle, streaming architecture, error handling, and concurrency model.
